title: Prosthetist
how_to_become: To become a prosthetist in the NHS, you must complete a pre-registration undergraduate degree programme approved by the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC).

salary.entry_note: Prosthetists in the NHS start at £33,247 before tax.
salary.experienced_note: Specialist prosthetists can earn up to £50,702. Advanced prosthetists can earn up to £59,159 before tax.

description: Prosthetists design and create artificial limbs for people who need them. They care for people who have arms or legs missing from birth or those who lose limbs through accident, injury, or amputation.
role_overview: As a prosthetist, you'll work closely with patients to understand their needs and goals, measure and model the remaining limb using plaster or scanning technology, and use computer-aided design to create prosthetic limbs. Once made by a prosthetic technician, you'll fit the limb to ensure comfort and performance. You'll work with doctors, nurses, physiotherapists, and occupational therapists to provide appropriate rehabilitation and aftercare.

entry_requirements.overview: A Prosthetics and Orthotics undergraduate degree at SCQF level 10 approved by the HCPC is required.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to college, university, or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.college_route: At college, you could do an HND in Engineering Systems at SCQF level 8.
education_and_training.widening_access: The Scottish Wider Access Programme (SWAP) supports adult learners with few or no qualifications to enter higher education. Many universities also provide access programmes for degree entry qualifications.
education_and_training.note: After graduation, you must register with the HCPC before applying for NHS vacancies. HCPC registration must be renewed every 2 years.

learning_and_development.overview: Prosthetists must keep skills and knowledge up to date through continuing professional development (CPD). HCPC registration must be renewed every 2 years.
cpd_providers: British Association of Prosthetists and Orthotists (BAPO)
International Society for Prosthetics and Orthotics (ISPO)

progression_roles: Head of orthotics and prosthetics service

professional_bodies: Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC) — mandatory for NHS practice
British Association of Prosthetists and Orthotists (BAPO)
International Society for Prosthetics and Orthotics (ISPO)

chunks.text: To become a prosthetist in the NHS, you must complete a pre-registration undergraduate degree in Prosthetics and Orthotics approved by the HCPC.
Prosthetists in the NHS start at £33,247 before tax. Specialist prosthetists can earn up to £50,702. Advanced prosthetists can earn up to £59,159 before tax.
Prosthetists design and create artificial limbs for people born with missing limbs or those who lose limbs through accident, injury, or amputation.

Prosthetists work with patients to understand their needs and goals, measure and model the remaining limb using plaster or scanning technology, use computer-aided design to create prosthetic limbs, and fit them to ensure comfort and performance. They collaborate with doctors, nurses, physiotherapists, and occupational therapists to provide rehabilitation and aftercare.
Useful school subjects include Biology, Engineering Science, Physics, Maths, and English. A Prosthetics and Orthotics undergraduate degree at SCQF level 10 approved by the HCPC is required.
Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to college, university, or a new job with NHSScotland.

In Scotland, the University of Strathclyde offers a 4-year pre-registration undergraduate programme in Prosthetics and Orthotics approved by the HCPC and ISPO. College options include HND in Engineering Systems at SCQF level 8. SWAP supports adult learners into higher education.

Prosthetists maintain skills through CPD including courses, conferences, and seminars provided by BAPO and ISPO. HCPC registration must be renewed every 2 years, confirming practice, proficiency, and CPD standards have been met.

Prosthetists can specialise in diabetes or working with children, and progress to senior, specialist, or head of orthotics and prosthetics service roles with team and budget responsibility. Teaching, research, and practice educator opportunities are also available.

Prosthetists must register with the HCPC to work in the NHS. They can also join the British Association of Prosthetists and Orthotists (BAPO) and the International Society for Prosthetics and Orthotics (ISPO).
